Whereas , in the year 1918 , the State appro-
priated the small sums of $ 3,000.00 annually
for the years 1918 and 1919 , and $5,000.00 for

for the benefit of the West Kentucky In-
dustrial College for Colored Persons , at Pa-
ducah , Kentucky , the former appropriations
based on the condition that said institution
should deed its buildings and grounds to the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , and ,
Whereas , the said institution did in the
year 1918 , by its board of trustees , execute
and deliver to the said Commonwealth its
real property , buildings and grounds to which
same the Commonwealth now holds the fee
simple title ; and ,
Whereas , it appears and is true that this
institution during its progress has prepared
more teachers who were able to pass the re-
quired examinations for State certificates , and
thereby qualified to teach in the schools of
the Commonwealth , than any other school at
a similar per capita expenses to the Com-
monwealth ;
Whereas , these teachers thus qualified are
now satisfactorily filling positions in numer-
ous schools in the rural districts of Western
Kentucky , as well as in many cities therein ,
including Hopkinsville , Mayfield , Paducah ,
and many other of the smaller cities , towns
and villages of Western Kentucky ; and ,
Whereas , since this college is thoroughly
well established , and is doing splendid serv-
ice in the instruction and qualification of
teachers , and is recognized as a valuable aid
to the school work of the Commonwealth ,
it would undoubtedly be no less than a calam-
ity to cease the support of this institution ,
and would be disastrous to the efforts of the
negro teachers of Western Kentucky , who

classification.title: An Act providing for a system of registration of every voter in the Commonwealth of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act establishes a comprehensive system for voter registration across Kentucky. It mandates the appointment of registration officers in every precinct, specifies the timing and methods for registration, and outlines voter qualifications, including residency requirements. The act also details the process for preparing registration forms, administering oaths, setting the pay for registration officers, notifying voters of registration times and places, and designates specific days for registration, amendment, and purging of voter lists. Penalties for violations and the right to appeal to the circuit court are also included. The act repeals several previous sections related to voter registration.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns voter registration, a key area targeted by Jim Crow laws. While race is not explicitly mentioned, voter registration laws during the Jim Crow era were often designed and implemented in ways that disenfranchised Black voters through discriminatory practices like poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses, which were often embedded within broader registration requirements. This statute's comprehensive nature and the historical context of voter registration in the South during this period strongly suggest an implicit racial dimension. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with an implicit racial indicator. The confidence is high due to the category and historical context, but human review is not strictly necessary as the implicit nature is well-supported by the context of voter registration laws in the Jim Crow South.

pointed need not be members or stockholders of the
association ; but shall have the same powers and
rights as other directors . Such directors shall not
number more than one-fifth of the entire number of
directors .

13 . ELECTION OF OFFICERS .

ident and one or more vice presidents . They shall

19. PURCHASING BUSINESS OF OTHER ASSOCIA-
TIONS , PERSONS , FIRMS OR CORPORATIONS- PAYMENT-
STOCK ISSUED .
Whenever an association , organized hereunder
with preferred capital stock , shall purchase the
stock or any property , or any interest in any prop-
erty of any person , firm or corporation or associa-
tion, it may discharge the obligations so incurred ,
wholly or in part , by exchanging for the acquired in-
terest , shares of its preferred capital stock to an
amount which at par value would equal the fair
market value of the stock or interest so purchased ,
as determined by the board of directors . In that
case the transfer to the association of the stock or
interest purchased shall be equivalent to payment in
cash for the shares of stock issued .
$ 20. ANNUAL REPORTS .
Each association formed under this act shall pre-
pare and make out an annual report on forms to be
furnished by the Dean of the College of Agriculture
of the University of Kentucky , containing the name
of the association ; its principal place of business ;
and a general statement of its business operations
during the fiscal year, showing the amount of capi-
tal stock paid up and the number of stockholders of
a stock association or the number of members and
amount of membership fees received , if a non - stock
association ; the total expenses of operations ; the
amount of its indebtedness or liabilities , and its bal-
ance sheets .
$ 21 . CONFLICTING LAWS NOT TO APPLY .
Any provisions of law which are in conflict with
this Act shall not be construed as applying to the
associations herein provided for .

36 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Chapter 38 of the Acts of the General As-
sembly of 1920 , relating to the election and qualifi-
cation of members of boards of trustees in graded
common school districts be and the same is hereby
repealed ; also Chapter 46 of the Acts of the General
Assembly of 1920 , relating to the establishment of
independent , consolidated , graded and high school ;
also Chapter 25 of the Acts of the General Assembly
of 1920 , relating to the condemnation of land by
boards of trustees of graded common school dis-
tricts ; also Section 4464 , Section 4500 , and all in-
tervening sections , of the Kentucky Statutes , Car-
roll's Edition , 1918 , relating to the management and
control of graded common school districts , be and
the same are hereby repealed ; and in lieu thereof
are now enacted the following sections to be known
and designated as :
Section 4464. Each graded common school dis-
trict organized and incorporated at the time this Act
goes into effect shall be under the management and
control of a board of trustees .
Section 4465. The board of trustees of each
graded common school district shall consist of five
(5 ) members , who shall be elected at large from the
qualified voters of the graded common school dis-
trict , as hereinafter provided for terms of three ( 3 )
years , beginning with the second Saturday of May
following their election , and shall hold office until
their succesors are elected and qualified . Members
of boards of trustees shall qualify by taking the oath
prescribed for public offices under Section 228 of
the Constitution of the State . Failure to qualify
within seven ( 7 ) days after election shall constitute
a vacancy .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 51
Section 244. Cases in which jury must be kept
together . On the trial of offenses which are or may
be punished capitally or by life imprisonment the
jurors after they are accepted , if all of same sex,
shall not be permitted to separate , but shall be kept
together , in charge of the proper officers . But if the
jury consists of both male and female , then the sexes
of such jury may be permitted to separate during
the trial and when necessary after final submission
of the case, each sex being kept together in charge of
an officer of like sex, as if two separate juries .
On the trial of other felonies the jurors before the
case is submitted to them, may be permitted to sep-
arate , in the discretion of the court, but after the
case is submitted they shall be kept together in
charge of officers . On the trial of misdemeanors the
jurors may be permitted to separate , or the court
may order them to be kept together .
Approved March 7, 1922 .

CHAPTER 29.
AN ACT to continue the existence of the Kentucky Council of
Defense for the sole purpose of completing the work of gather-
ing, arranging and preserving the history of Kentucky's part
in the World War , making an appropriation for that work,
and providing for final disposition of its war historical records .
Whereas , the Kentucky Council of Defense was continued in
existence for two years from the end of the 1920 session of the
General Assembly of Kentucky for the sole purpose of complet-
ing the work of gathering , arranging and preserving the history
of Kentucky's part in the World War , and whereas , it appears
from the report of said Council that it has been impossible for it
to complete this historical work for the reason that the War De-
partment at Washington has not yet sent to the State of Ken-
tucky , about 15,000 statements of service of Kentucky men in
the army, navy and other branches , and will probably not send
such records for several months , and whereas , it has been im-
possible in the past two years to collect and preserve all of the -
historical data which should be collected and preserved showing
the part taken by Kentucky men and women in the World War ,
and whereas the funds heretofore appropriated to said Council
for this historical work , will not be sufficient to enable it to
complete the work .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Kentucky Council of Defense ,
created by act approved March 15 , 1918 , and con-
tinued in existence by act of March 23 , 1920 , for two
years from the end of the 1920 session of the Gen-
eral Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , be
continued in existence for an additional period of two
years from the end of the 1922 session of the Gen-
eral Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
for the sole purpose of completing the work of gath-
ering, arranging and preserving all material relating
to Kentucky's part in the World War , and to that
end said council shall have power and it shall be its
duty to employ and fix the compensation of such

150 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
1a . Each county district in the Commonwealth
of Kentucky at the time this act goes into effect shall
be under the management and control of a county
board of education , consisting of five members to
be nominated and elected as hereinafter provided
for .
§ 1b. It shall be the duty of the county board of
education of each county in this state , serving at the
time this act goes into effect , as soon as practicable
after this act goes into effect but not later than
July 1 , 1923 , to meet and to divide their respective
county school district into five divisions as nearly
equal in population as is practicable , and to cause
boundaries to be made for such division which shall
be recorded in a book to be kept in the office of the
county board of education ; provided , that divisions
shall contain so far as practicable integral voting
precincts .
Any citizen may except to the action of the board
in forming any division , and shall have a right to
be heard ; and if any citizen residing in any such
division shall feel himself aggrieved he shall have
a right to prosecute an appeal to the county court
and from the county court to the circuit court in
which such county is located . The procedure on ap-
peals shall be the same as in other civil cases .
$ 1c . The county board of education in each
county in this Commonwealth , after such counties
have been laid off into five divisions as herein di-
rected , shall be elected every two years by the quali-
fied voters of such divisions , each division being
entitled to elect one member , as herein provided .
The first election under this act shall be held at
the regular November election in 1923 , and the mem-
bers so elected shall qualify and assume the duties
of the office on the first Monday in January , 1924 .

with are hereby repealed .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 157
1
§ 1. That all papers constituting a discharge of
any soldier, sailor , marine or other officer , agent
or employee , of the United States Army or Navy
or other military or naval establishment , which are
given, executed or delivered to such soldier , sailor ,
marine , or other officer, agent or employee , evidenc-
ing his discharge from the service of the United
States and which shows the unit or part of such de-
partment to which such person was attached and
from which he was discharged , are hereby declared
to be recordable instruments and may be recorded
by the county court clerk of the county in which
the person so discharged is a resident , and upon
presentation of such discharge papers it is the duty
of the county court clerk to record the same . No
charge may be made by the clerk for recording such
discharge .
§ 2. In any proceeding in the courts of this State
in which such discharge paper may come in question
and in which it could be used as legal evidence of
any fact , it shall be competent as evidence in any
and all such proceedings to use an attested or certi-
fied copy of such discharge .
§ 3. Any such discharges as may have hereto-
fore been recorded in substantial compliance with
the provisions of this Act are hereby declared valid
to the same extent and for the same purposes as if
they had been recorded under the provisions of this
Act .
§ 4. The county court clerk shall provide a suita-
ble record book for such registration .
Approved March 22 , 1922 .

AN ACT to provide for the commitment . government and con-
duct of delinquent children committed to the Houses of Re-
form during their confinement in said Houses of Reform and
while on parole , and declaring an emergency .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. All commitments by county courts or other
courts having power to commit minors to the Houses
of Reform shall be until such minor shall arrive at
the age of twenty - one .
§ 2. A delinquent child committed to the Houses
of Reform by a court other than a circuit court shall
not be allowed to be released from the Houses of
Reform except :
(a ) Upon the arrival at the age of twenty -one ;
(b) Upon being discharged by the State Board
of Charities and Corrections ;
(c ) Upon being paroled by the State Board of
Charities and Corrections .
§ 3. A delinquent child committed to the Houses
of Reform by a court other than a circuit court may
be allowed to go out on parole at such time and on
such terms as shall be prescribed by rules of the
State Board of Charities and Corrections , and not
otherwise ; and the said board shall have the right
to apprehend a paroled child who , in its judgment ,
should be returned to the Houses of Reform .
§ 4. Where a minor is committed to the Houses
of Reform for the duration of his minority , by a
circuit court, after having been sentenced upon a
conviction in said court , the State Board of Chari-
ties and Corrections shall have the right to grant to
such minor a parole in the same manner as is above
provided, except that such parole must have the

CHAPTER 51 .
AN ACT establishing the fiscal year of the counties of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That , beginning with the 1st day of January , 1923 ,
the fiscal year of each county of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky shall begin on the 1st day of January
and end on the 31st day of December of each year :
Provided , however , that the current fiscal year of
the several counties of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky which will otherwise end on the thirtieth day
of June, nineteen hundred and twenty -two , shall be ,
and it is hereby , extended so that it shall end on the
thirty-first day of December nineteen hundred and
twenty-two .

classification.title: An Act relating to life or endowment insurance policies.
classification.summary: This act mandates that all life or endowment insurance policies issued in Kentucky, with certain exceptions, must include a provision detailing the insured's entitlement to a stipulated form of insurance in the event of premium default after three years. The net value of this insurance must be at least equal to the policy's reserve, less specified deductions. It also outlines conditions for calculating reserves for intermediate and higher-risk policies.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns regulations for life and endowment insurance policies, specifically regarding provisions for premium default and reserve calculations. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. The provisions apply universally to all policyholders. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 57 .
AN ACT repealing Section 807 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
1915 Edition , and re -enacting the same .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 807 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
Edition , 1915 , be and the same is hereby repealed
and the following enacted in lieu thereof :
Any person who shall wilfully and maliciously tear
up , displace , break or disturb any rail or other fix-
ture attached to the track or switch of any railroad
in operation or break any bridge or viaduct of such
road , or who shall place any obstruction on the track
or switch of such road or do any act whereby any
engine or car might be upset , arrested or thrown
from the track of such road or switch , or any bridge
or turnout , shall be confined in the penitentiary not
less than five nor more than twenty years . In the
event death shall result to any person whosoever
from the acts herein prohibited , the person guilty
under this act shall be guilty of murder .
Approved March 23 , 1922 .

classification.title: AN ACT repealing Section 807 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's 1915 Edition , and re -enacting the same .
classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 807 of the Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's Edition, 1915. The re-enacted section criminalizes the wilful and malicious act of damaging or obstructing railroad tracks, switches, bridges, or viaducts. Penalties include confinement in the penitentiary for not less than five nor more than twenty years, and guilt of murder if death results from the prohibited acts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes an act that criminalizes acts of sabotage against railroads. It defines specific offenses related to damaging or obstructing railroad infrastructure and outlines severe penalties, including imprisonment and potential murder charges if death occurs. is located . Upon the approval of said petition by
the board of education of such city and by the county

make an order on its record books providing for the

once .

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 193
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for a Board to propose plans for the construction, furnishing, and equipment of a hospital. The plans are submitted to the mayor, who then presents them to the General Council. The General Council has the power to adopt or reject the Board's recommended plan with a two-thirds vote. If rejected, alternative plans can be considered. If no plan is approved within a specified timeframe, the Board, with the approval of the mayor and fiscal court, can choose a plan. The General Council cannot alter any proposed plan but must adopt it in its entirety.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 20 and Section 128 of an Act Entitled "An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 117 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Cities of the First Class, Approved March 17, 1896."
classification.summary: This act amends two sections of a previous act concerning cities of the first class. Section 20 is amended to allow the general council to impose fines not exceeding one hundred dollars, or imprisonment not exceeding fifty days, or both, for designated misdemeanors not covered by general laws. It also allows for increased fines by ordinance if the general statutes impose a fine not exceeding one hundred dollars. Section 128 is amended to change the maximum imprisonment from thirty days to fifty days and the maximum fine from fifty dollars to one hundred dollars for cases tried without a jury, unless the right to a jury is waived.

CHAPTER 61.
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Section 1160 , Kentucky Statutes ,
Carroll's Edition , 1915 , relating to assault with intent to rob .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section Eleven Hundred Sixty ( 1160 ) , Ken-
tucky Statutes , Carroll's Edition , 1915 , be amended
and re -enacted so that when so amended and re-
enacted same shall read as follows :
If any person , with an offensive weapon or in-
strument , shall unlawfully and maliciously assault ,
or in or by any forcible and violent manner , de-
mand any money , goods or chattels , bond , bill , deed
or will , or other evidences of right , or other thing
of value of or from any other person , with an intent
to rob or commit a robbery upon such person , he
shall be confined in the penitentiary not less than
five nor more than twenty years .
Approved March 23 , 1922 .

CHAPTER 72 .
AN ACT to promote the production of better eggs in Kentucky
by regulating the traffic in eggs to prevent fraud and misrepre-
sentation in dealing in eggs , to prevent the sale of eggs unfit
for human food , and to fix penalties for violation of the pro-
visions thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. No person , firm or corporation engaged in
the buying and selling of eggs shall sell , offer or ex-
pose for sale , or traffic in , any eggs unfit for human
food , unless the same is broken in shell and dena-
tured so that it cannot be used for human food.
For the purpose of this Act , an egg shall be deemed
unfit for human food if it be addled or moldy , has
a black rot , a white rot , or a blood ring , or if it has
an adherent yolk or a bloody or green white ; or if
it be incubated beyond the blood ring stage ; or if it
be composed in whole or in part of a filthy , decom-
posed or putrid substance .
§ 2. That between May 15 of each year and Jan-
uary 15 of the following year no person , firm or
corporation engaged in the buying and selling of
eggs , shall buy or sell eggs without candling them ,
and no payment either in cash or merchandise shall
be made for those unfit for food . No person , firm
or corporation shall, in buying or selling eggs , take
or give a greater or less dockage for eggs unfit for

Section 11. That any person manufacturing anti-
hog cholera serum , hog cholera virus , or any other
product used in the prevention or treatment of swine
diseases , shall before any of said products are sold
or offered for sale in this State , pay or cause to be
paid to the chairman of the board an annual license
fee of fifty dollars and shali execute bond in the
sum of five thousand dollars for their faithful com-
pliance with the laws , rules and regulations of the
board , said bond to be acceptable to the chairman of
the board . All licenses or forfeitures under this
Act shall be paid to the chairman and shall be used
in the same manner as the funds appropriated for
the use of the board . Any person violating the pro-
visions of this section shall upon conviction be fined
not less than five hundred dollars nor more than
five thousand dollars . Any veterinarian , or other
person , who knowingly purchases or uses anti -hog
cholera serum , hog cholera virus , or any other prod-
uct used in the prevention or treatment of swine
diseases , manufactured and sold by any person , who
has not complied with the provisions of this section ,
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and upon conviction
shall be fined not less than ten dollars nor more than
fifty dollars for each offense . Provided , however ,
that nothing in this Act shall be construed to pro-
hibit any pharmacist , regularly licensed under the
laws of the State of Kentucky from selling in this
State anti -hog cholera serum , hog cholera virus , or
any other product used in the prevention or treat-
ment of swine diseases , which have been government
inspected and approved , provided said serum , virus
or product is sold by a manufacturing concern li-
censed under the provisions of this section to sell
same in Kentucky .

by ordinance ; and said judge shall not receive any

amining trials in felony cases . No person shall be
eligible to the office of police judge who , at the time
of election , is not twenty- five years of age , a resi-
dent of the city for four years . This shall not apply
to present incumbents nor to police judges elected
in November , one thousand eight hundred and nine-
ty-three .

$ 10 . This Act is declared to be remedial ; its pur-
pose is to make courts more serviceable to the peo-
ple by way of settling controversies , and affording
relief from uncertainty and insecurity with respect
to rights , duties and relations , and is to be liberally
interpreted and administered .
11. The word " person " wherever used in this
Act , shall be construed to mean any person , part-
nership , joint stock company , incorporated associa-
tion , or society , or municipal or other corporation
of any character whatsoever .
§ 12. All statutes , or laws in conflict or inconsis-
tent with the provisions of this Act , are hereby re-
pealed . It is intended that this Act shall be valid
to the fullest extent possible ; and that the invalidity ,
if any , of any part or feature thereof, shall not af-
fect or render the remainder of the Act invalid , or
inoperative .
13. This Act shall take effect from and after its
passage .
Approved March 23, 1922 .

CHAPTER 84 .
AN ACT to amend Section 5 of Chapter 17 of the Acts of the
General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , 1920 , re-
lating to roads and bridges , by making an addition to Project
No. 6 .
Whereas , Project No. 6 of the Primary System of State High-
ways of Kentucky , and the Virginia system of State Highways
approach within three miles of each other at Jenkins , in Letcher
County , Kentucky , and
Whereas , the Virginia system of State Highways has been ex-
tended to Pound Gap on the Pine Mountain at the Kentucky -Vir-
ginia State line , a distance approximately of three miles from
Jenkins , Kentucky , which is located on Project No. 6, and
Whereas , the Pine Mountain has always been practically an
impassable barrier between Kentucky and Virginia for about
125 miles , and
Whereas , the Lonesome Pine Trail leading from Knoxville ,
Tennessee , to Ashland , Kentucky , has been located through
Pound Gap , and all of said Trail except the three mile gap herein
provided for is already a part of the Tennessee -Virginia or Ken-
tucky highways systems , and
Whereas , it is of paramount importance not only to the ad-
joining counties of Virginia and Kentucky , but also to the gen-
eral traveling public that this section of road be made a part of
the Kentucky State Highway System ; therefore ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the section of road leading from Project No.
6 at Jenkins , in Letcher County , Kentucky , to Pound
Gap , on the Kentucky -Virginia State line, be added
to said Project No. 6 and be made a part of the
Primary System of State Highways .
Approved March 23 , 1922 .

CHAPTER 90.
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Chapter 93 of the Acts of the
General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , of 1920 ,
entitled : "An Act relating to revenue and taxation , requiring
an excise or license tax to be paid on all gasoline sold at re-
tail in this State , and requiring any person engaging in busi-
ness of selling gasoline at retail to pay said tax for the priv-
ilege of engaging in said business ; providing the manner of
collecting said tax , requiring reports and fixing fines and penal-
ties for violations of the act."
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Chapter 93 of the Acts of the General As-
sembly of 1920 be , and the same is hereby amended
and re - enacted so that said chapter , when so amend-
ed and re -enacted , shall read as follows :
§ 1. All persons , corporations and associations
engaged in Kentucky in the business of selling at re-
tail gasoline , benzol , naphtha or other motor fuels
commonly used in internal combustion engines , ex-
cept kerosene , fuel oil or crude oil , shall pay a license
tax of one cent on each gallon so sold and the said
tax shall be for use in the construction and main-
tenance of State highways , and shall be set apart in
the State Treasury to the credit of the State road
fund .
§ 2. The State Tax Commission is hereby au-
thorized and empowered to prescribe and furnish
all blanks , forms , books and reports necessary in
carrying out the provisions of this act .

teen juridical days ; second Monday in March and

CHAPTER 97 .
AN ACT to amend Section 1159 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
1915 Edition , relating to burglary and robbery .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 1159 , Kentucky Statutes , Corroll's
1915 Edition , be amended and re -enacted , so that
when so amended and re -enacted said section shall
real as follows :
Every person guilty of burglary shall be punished
with death , or confinement in the penitentiary for
life , in the discretion of the jury . This act shall not
affect the statute or law of the State denouncing any
penalty for robbery not accompanied by an act of
burglary .
Whereas, the crimes of burglary are of almost
daily occurrence , and the present punishment pro-
vided by law is inadequate , and ineffective to deter
criminals , an emergency is hereby declared to exist
and this act shall take effect from and after its ap-
proval by the Governor .
Approved March 24, 1922 .

CHAPTER 100 .
AN ACT for the benefit of the West Kentucky Industrial Col-
lege , established at Paducah , Kentucky , making appropriations
thereto .
Whereas , in the year 1918 , the State appropriated the small
sums of $3,000.00 annually for the years 1918 and 1919 , and
$5,000.00 for the years 1920 and 1921 , each respectively , for the
benefit of the West Kentucky Industrial College for Colored Per-
sons , at Paducah , Kentucky , the former appropriations based on
the condition that said institution should deed its buildings and
grounds to the Commonwealth of Kentucky , and ,
Whereas , the said institution did in the year 1918 , by its board
of trustees , execute and deliver to the said Commonwealth its
real property, buildings and grounds , to which same the Com-
monwealth now holds the fee simple title ; and ,
Whereas , it appears and is true that this institution during its
progress has prepared more teachers who were able to pass the
required examinations for State certificates , and thereby quali-
fied to teach in the schools of the Commonwealth , than any other
school at a similar per capita expense to the Commonwealth ;
Whereas , these teachers thus qualified are now satisfactorily
filling positions in numerous schools in the rural districts of
Western Kentucky , as well as in many cities therein , including
Hopkinsville , Mayfield , Paducah , and many other of the smaller
cities , towns and villages of Western Kentucky ; and ,
Whereas , since this college is thoroughly well established , and
is doing splendid service in the instruction and qualification of
teachers , and is recognized as a valuable aid to the school work
of the Commonwealth , it would undoubtedly be no less than a
calamity to cease the support of this institution , and would be
disastrous to the efforts of the negro teachers of Western Ken-
tucky , who have begun preparation for better teaching service ,
to be compelled to abandon their already well advanced work ,
and would also be unfair to those of Western Kentucky citizens
who have heretofore given of their time and means toward the
establishment of the college , to leave the institution " suspended
in mid -air " without any reasonably fair appropriation , which ap-
pears to have been done in omitting it from the "Budget Bill ; "
and,
Whereas , the small appropriations above mentioned were in-
sufficient to put the institution on the basis to which it is justly
entitled , but enough to have the effect of retarding any assist-
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1922, p. 319

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

§ 1. That the term common carrier or common
carriers whenever used in this Act, shall mean and
embrace any person , corporation or company en-
gaged in the transportation of goods or pasengers
for hire or compensation within this State by rail or
boat , however propelled , but shall not embrace the

sleeping car companies , chair car companies , and the
word carrier whenever used in this Act shall mean
and include common carriers , except street railroad

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 4023, Article 1, Chapter 108, Kentucky Statutes, Volume 3, Carroll's Edition of 1918, relating to the general revenue laws of the State of Kentucky.
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 4023 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the liability for property taxes. It clarifies that if property is sold before January 1st of the tax year, the purchaser is responsible for the taxes, and if sold after January 1st, the seller is responsible, in the absence of a contrary contract. The original section states that the holder of legal title, equitable title, or claimant/bailee in possession on July 1st of the assessment year is liable for taxes. It also provides an exemption for administrators, executors, trustees, etc., residing in the state from liability for taxes on intangible personal property if the beneficial owner resides outside the state, with an exception for personal representatives during estate settlement for non-resident legatees.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the allocation of property tax liability between buyers and sellers based on the date of sale and the original liability of title holders and possessors. CHAPTER 106 .
AN ACT making it a felony for any grain , produce or cotton or
stock broker, to receive deposits as margins , for the purchase
of grain , produce , cotton or stock , from any person , firm or
corporation , when it is known that said brokerage firm , cor-
poration or individual is insolvent , and prescribing a penalty
therefor.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That any officer , manager or employee of any firm ,
corporation or individuals engaged in the brokerage
business , as hereinafter defined , who shall receive or
assent to the receiving of deposits in cash , securities
or other things of value from any one, person , firm
or corporation , as a customer or dealer , to be used
as margins for the purchase of stocks , grain , produce
or cotton or for the outright purchase thereof when
such officer , manager or employee knows or has rea-
sonable grounds for believing that such person , firm
or corporation so engaged in the brokerage busi-
ness is insolvent , shall be personally liable for such
deposits or margins so received , and shall be guilty
of a felony and upon conviction shall be punished
by confinement in the penitentiary for not less than
one nor more than ten years .

CHAPTER 110 .
AN ACT to provide for the establishment and operation of credit
unions in the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Incorporation and By -laws . Any eight per-
sons , residents of the State of Kentucky , may apply
to the head of the Department of Banking for per-
mission to organize a credit union . Persons uniting
to form a credit union shall sign and acknowledge in
duplicate articles of incorporation and shall enter
into articles of agreement , in which they shall bind
themselves to comply with its requirements and with
all the laws , rules and regulations applicable to
credit unions . The certificate of organization shall
contain the following :
( 1 ) The name of the proposed credit union .
( 2) A statement that incorporation is desired
under this particular law .
(3) The par value of shares , which shall be five
dollars .
(4) The city , town or village in which the busi-
ness office of the corporation is to be located .
(5 ) The names and addresses of the subscribers
to the articles of incorporation and a statement of
the number of shares of stock subscribed by each .

tucky .
$ 20. Loans to Members . A credit union may
loan to its members for such purposes and upon such
security and terms as the by-laws shall provide and
the credit committee shall approve ; but security
must be taken for any loan in excess of fifty dollars .

A borrower may repay the whole or any part of

No director or member of the committee shall be

At the close of each fiscal year there shall be set

§ 22. Dividends . At the close of the fiscal year
a credit union may declare a dividend from the net
earnings . Dividends shall be paid on all fully paid
shares outstanding at the close of the fiscal year , but
shares which became fully paid during the year shall
be entitled to a proportional part of said dividends
calculated from the first day of the month following
such payment in full .
$ 23 . Notes -Drafts-Bills of Exchange . Notes ,
drafts , bills of exchange executed for the purpose of
this act , having a maturity not to exceed six months ,
and indorsed by a national bank , a state bank or a
trust company may be re -discounted in the open
market . The total of such paper outstanding shall
at no time exceed the paid in capital and surplus ,
provided , however , that the head of the Department
of Banking , in his discretion , may extend this limit
temporarily , and provided further , that the limita-
tion here fixed shall not be considered money bor-
rowed under Section 18 .

$ 26. Change of Place of Business . A credit
union may change its place of business on written no-
tice to the head of the Department of Banking .
§ 27. Taxation . A credit union shall be deemed
an institution for savings , and together with all
the accumulations therein shall not be subject to
taxation , except as to real estate owned . The shares
of credit unions shall not be subject to any stock
transfer tax, either when issued or when transferred
from one member to another .
$ 28. Repeal of Conflicting Legislation . All laws
and parts of laws in conflict herewith repealed . It
is intended that this Act shall be valid to the fullest
possible extent , and the invalidity of any part or
section thereof shall not affect or render the re-
mainder of the Act invalid or inoperative .

CHAPTER 113 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Section 1199 , Kentucky Statutes ,
Carroll's Edition of 1915 , providing punishment for receiving
stolen goods .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 1199 , Kentucky Statutes , Car-
roll's Edition of 1915 , be , and the same is hereby
amended by adding , at the close thereof , the follow-
ing words :
99
" The possession of any stolen goods shall be
prima facie evidence of the guilt , under this section ,
of any person or persons having such possession .
And said section , as thus amended , is now re-
enacted so as to read as follows :
"Section 1199. Receiving Stolen Goods . Who-
ever shall receive any stolen goods , chattels or other
thing , the stealing whereof is punished as a felony
or misdemeanor , knowing the same to be stolen , shall
be liable to the same punishment to which the per-
son stealing the same is , by law , subjected . Such of-
fenders may be convicted , though the principal of-
fender has not been convicted . The possession of
any stolen goods shall be prima facie evidence of the
guilt , under this section , of any person or persons
having such possession . "
§ 2. That all laws, and parts of laws , inconsistent ,
or in conflict , herewith , are hereby repealed .
Approved March 24 , 1922 .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1922, p. 404

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 1199, Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's Edition of 1915, providing punishment for receiving stolen goods.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1199 of the Kentucky Statutes to establish that the possession of any stolen goods is prima facie evidence of guilt for receiving stolen goods. It reiterates the punishment for receiving stolen goods, which is the same as for the person who stole them, and allows for conviction of the receiver even if the principal offender has not been convicted.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the criminal offense of receiving stolen goods and establishes a legal presumption of guilt based on possession. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets any racial group, nor does it create separate provisions or penalties based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' passed by the General Assembly in 1922. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and unrelated to racial discrimination.

AN ACT to amend an act relating to revenue and taxation , and
entitled : "An Act relating to revenue and taxation , creating
a State Tax Commission , providing for the appointment , quali-
fication and term of office of its members , prescribing its
powers and duties , appropriating money therefor , providing
for the assessment of property for taxation , and the super-
vision and equalization of assessments , and prescribing the
powers and duties of county clerks , county tax commissioners ,
sheriffs , collectors , Railroad Commission , Auditor of Public
Accounts, and other officers performing some duty in refer-
ence to the taxation of property , creating county boards of
supervisors , a State Board of Equalization and a State Board
of Valuation and Assessment , and defining their powers and
duties , prescribing penalties , and repealing existing statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 1 of Subdivision 1 of Chapter 1
of an Act of the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , approved April 11 , 1917 , Special
Session Laws , 1917 , be amended by repealing said
Section 1 and inserting in lieu thereof the following ,
so that when said section is amended by substituting
and re -enacting in lieu thereof , the same shall read
as follows :
Section 1. Membership and Qualification .- There
shall be a State Tax Commission composed of three
commissioners , two of whom shall be from one of the
two dominant political parties , and one from the
other dominant political party . The members shall
be electors of the State , freeholders , and not less
than thirty years of age .

§ 5. That Section 14 of Subdivision 3 of Chapter
1 of an Act of the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , approved April 11 , 1917 ,
Special Session Laws , 1917 , be amended by repeal-
ing said Section 14 and inserting in lieu thereof the
following , so that when said section is so amended
by substituting and re -enacting in lieu thereof, the
following , the same shall read as follows :
Section 14. County Clerks - Report of Sale . On
or before July tenth of each year, each county clerk
shall prepare on blanks which shall be furnished by
the State Tax Commission , and shall file with the
State Tax Commission , a tabulated statement of all
sales of real estate as shown by the deed books of
his county for one year immediately preceding July
first , or for such other period as the State Tax Com-
mission may require .
The said statement shall contain the list of all
sales of real estate , town lots being kept separate
from other real estate , the price paid , with terms and
conditions , the number of acres and the assessed
value of same for the previous year . Where less
than a fee simple title is given said statement shall
show the interest conveyed ; and if only a part of a
tract of land or a town lot is sold it shall give a short
description of same and state what proportion it
bears to said tract or lot.
Said statement shall be sworn to by said clerk and
the county attorney , and the latter in his affidavit
shall state that he has examined the recorded and
unrecorded conveyances filed for said time and that
each and every conveyance , with the consideration
therefor and the assessed values , is correctly given
in said statement .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1922, p. 408

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 14 of Subdivision 3 of Chapter 1 of an Act of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, approved April 11, 1917, Special Session Laws, 1917.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the reporting of real estate sales by county clerks. It requires county clerks to file a tabulated statement of all real estate sales with the State Tax Commission annually. The statement must detail the price, terms, acreage, and assessed value of properties, separating town lots from other real estate. It also requires reporting of less than fee simple titles and descriptions of partial sales. The statement must be sworn to by the county clerk and county attorney.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 353
Section 25. Time of Sessions -Duties and Com-
pensation of Clerk . The board of supervisors shall
continue in session at its first meeting in each year
not less than two nor more than twelve days in coun-
ties not containing a city of the first , second , third ,
fourth or fifth class ; in counties having therein a
city of the first class , the board may remain in ses-
sion fifty days ; in counties having therein a city of
the second class the board may remain in session
forty days ; in counties having therein cities of the
third , fourth and fifth class the board may remain in
session twenty-five days . The clerk of the county
court shall act as clerk of the board of supervisors .
He shall be allowed the same compensation for his
services as a member of the board , and be paid in the
same way. It shall be the duty of the said clerk to
prepare and deliver to the sheriff a list of the names
of all persons whose property has been raised by
the board of supervisors , and the amount thereof .
§ 9. It is the intention of the Legislature in enact-
ing this law to enact each section and subsection
thereof separately and specifically , and if any section
or any subsection thereof shall be held invalid or
unconstitutional the same shall not be held to affect
in any wise any other section or subsection of this
act, but they shall remain in full force and effect .
All laws and parts of laws in conflict herewith are
hereby repealed .
Approved March 24 , 1922 .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That there is hereby created an efficiency
commission consisting of four members to be ap-
pointed by the Governor, not more than two of whom
shall belong to one political party . Said original
appointments shall be confirmed by the Senate .
§ 2. Vacancies in the commission from any cause
shall be filled by the Governor by the appointment
of a person or persons named by the commission ,
and the commission shall name such persons from
the political party in which the vacancy occurred .
§ 3. The commission so appointed is directed and
authorized to make a general survey of, and inquire
into all departments , commissions , and boards of the
State Government , as well as all institutions sup-
ported in whole or in part by public funds of the
State , and shall make reports and recommendations
touching their conditions and needs and matters re-
lating to the curtailment of expenses and increased
efficiency . In addition to these the scope of its
powers and duties shall extend to an inquiry into
and a report and recommendation concerning every
branch and arm of the State Government that is con-
tributed to or supported in whole or in part by State
funds or that performs judicial , executive , legisla-
tive or ministerial functions . It shall also inquire
into and report concerning all existing and prospec-
tive sources of revenue .
§ 4. It shall have power to administer oaths , to
summon to appear before it any person who in
its
judgment can furnish useful information touching

CHAPTER 117 .
AN ACT relating to public safety ; providing for certain devices
to be used in the operation of motion picture machines , and
a penalty for the violation of the law .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Each and every person , firm , corporation ,
partnership , or association engaged in the business
of operating a motion picture theater , or displaying
moving pictures , in this Commonwealth , shall at all
times use only machines of approved design , hav-
ing both reels enclosed in approved magazines con-
structed of metal (free from the use of solder ) hav-
ing spring -hinged self- closing doors in front side ,
equipped with a substantial latch and so arranged
as not to permit the entrance of flame . Each and
every machine , irrespective of how and where used
shall be equipped with a circuit breaker device , which
said device shall have the approval and endorsement
of the Underwriters ' Laboratories , incorporated , and
such that will stop the motor and automatically cut
off the heat and power in case of a breakage of the
film or film belt . Such device shall at all times be so
arranged as to accomplish the purpose above set out .
§ 2. Any person , firm , corporation , partnership
or association , their agents or servants operating a
moving picture machine without complying with the
provisions set out in paragraph 1 ( one ) hereof , shall
be guilty of misdemeanor , and upon conviction
shall be fined not less than one hundred nor more
than one thousand dollars , or confined in the county
jail not less than one nor more than six months , or
both so fined and imprisoned within the maximum ,
at the discretion of the jury .
Approved March 24 , 1922 .

ing shall be used for any occupancy other than dry

The statute also specifies construction standards for dry cleaning facilities, such as fire-resistive design, single-story height, and specific wall materials, and prohibits other occupancies within these buildings. The State Fire Marshal is empowered to refuse, suspend, or revoke permits for fraud or violations of state law.

§ 16. That all dry cleaning , washing, extracting
and redistilling shall be carried on in closed ma-
chines , which shall be fluid tight . In wash rooms
only the necessary appliances for washing, extract-
ing and re -distilling shall be permitting , but this
shall not be construed as prohibiting the use within .
wash room of drying tumblers of other than the
blower type. Tumblers of the suction type shall be
equipped with means for the admission of steam
within the cylinders . Washers and extractors shall
have hinged doors , and shall have attached to cover
fusible links so that in case of explosion the door
will automatically close ; such machines shall also
be provided with means for the relieving of any
undue pressure that may be generated within ma-
chine when cover is closed . The transfer of all
liquids shall be through continuous piping ; pipe con-
nections or threaded joints shall be made up with
litharge and glycerine , and all outlets or drain lines
shall be drained by gravity to settling or storage
tanks . Construction shall be such that there will be
no exposed liquid issuing from clarifyer or distill ,
but this shall not be construed as prohibiting prop-
erly guarded glass in sight feed for clarifyers . Glass
gauges shall be protected against mechanical injury
by means of suitable guards , and shall be equipped
with valves arranged to close automatically in case
of breakage of glass . No dry cleaning liquid shall
be settled in any open or unprotected vessels or
tanks . All piping and all metallic parts of each ma-
chine shall be properly grounded by at least No. 10
copper insulated wire to a water pipe or other
grounded device ; belting , if used , shall be grounded
by means of collector rings , installed on shafting .

classification.title: An Act to Regulate Dry Cleaning and Dyeing Establishments
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for dry cleaning and dyeing businesses, focusing on fire safety. It mandates that flammable liquids like naphtha, benzine, benzol, or light petroleum/coal-tar products can only be installed or redistilled in fire-resistive rooms. The act includes provisions for existing businesses, requiring them to conform to the new standards if reconstructed or rebuilt. It also states that discontinuing the business for three consecutive months in a non-conforming building constitutes abandonment, requiring full compliance before resuming operations. The State Fire Marshal is responsible for inspecting and approving buildings, structures, and equipment used in these businesses.

in which is located the road over which such load is
proposed to be hauled , and such permit must specify
the load to be hauled and the point from which and
to which it may be carried, but where the load is to
be hauled over roads lying in more than one county ,
it shall be necessary to secure a permit from the
County Judge , or nearest magistrate of each county
through which the load is to be moved .
3. Any person , firm, association or corporation
violating any of the provisions of this act shall be
deemed guilty of a misdemeanor , and , upon convic-
tion , shall be fined in any sum not less than Twenty-
five Dollars ( $25.00 ) , and , not more than Two Hun-
dred Dollars ( $ 200.00 ) , and , in addition thereto , any
person , firm , association or corporation violating
any of the provisions of this act shall be responsible
in the civil courts for any damage done to any of the
public roads of the State or the county . It shall be
the duty of the County Attorney of the county in
which such damage occurs to institute such actions
as may be necessary to recover any damage so oc-
casioned . Neither approved nor disapproved .

378 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 584 , Kentucky Statutes , ( Carroll's
1915 Edition ) , be and the same is hereby amended
by eliminating the words twelve per cent , five per
cent and fifteen per cent , wherever same appear in
said section , and substituting therefor the words
seven per cent , three per cent and ten per cent , so
that said section , when amended and re -enacted , will
read as follows :
Section 584. Each bank and trust company , or-
ganized under the laws of this Commonwealth and
authorized by law to receive deposits , shall keep on
hand , at all times , at least seven per cent of its total
demand deposits and three per cent of its total time
and savings deposits ; and , in cities which are reserve
or central reserve cities , under the act of Congress
of December 23 , 1913 , known as the Federal Reserve
Act , at least ten per cent in reserve cities and thir-
teen per cent in central reserve cities , of its total
demand deposits and three per cent of its total time
and savings deposits ; one-third of which reserve
shall be in money and the remainder may be in bal-
ances due from other banks and subject to call.
Demand deposits , within the meaning of this section ,
shall comprise all deposits payable within thirty
days , and time deposits shall comprise all deposits
payable after thirty days and all savings accounts
and certificates of deposit which are subject to not
less than thirty days ' notice before payment . Noth-
ing in this section shall be construed as authorizing
to receive deposits or do a banking business any
institution not authorized to do so by other pro-
visions of law .
The following resolution was adopted at the

twenty -eighth annual meeting of the Kentucky
Bankers Association , September 8 , 1920 , towit :
"Whereas , under existing laws of Kentucky ,
State Banks are required to maintain larger re-
serves than National and Federal Reserve Member
Banks , while all are subject to exactly the same busi-
ness requirements and conditions , and
Whereas , It would be a benefit to both the public
and State Banks and no burden on or injury to other
banks , but an act of equal and deserved justice , if
the reserves required of them were the same as those
required of National and Federal Reserve Member
Banks ; Therefore
Be it Resolved , That it is the sense of this Conven-
tion that the State laws should be so changed as to
make the reserves of State Banks the same as those
required of National and Federal Reserve Member
Banks ; and to that end , the officers and Legislative
Committee of this Association are hereby directed
to use their best efforts and influence at the next
session of the Kentucky Legislature . "
Neither approved nor disapproved .

thereof that does not occur within five years from
the date thereof. Provided that the City Treasurer
shall give the owner of the bond proposed to be re-

letter . It shall be the duty of the owner of any bond ,

changes therein, and the Auditor will keep a record

his records . The notice herein provided for shall

given , the notice shall be directed to such owner at
his last known address , and the bonds specified
therein shall bear no interest after the date fixed for
its redemption , and this provision shall be shown in
substance on the bonds . Mailing such registered let-
ter twenty days before the time fixed for such re-
demption shall be deemed a compliance herewith .
After the issue of such bonds no suit shall lie to
enjoin or resist the collection of any assessment or
tax in anticipation of which the bonds are issued ,
and the validity of the same shall not be questioned ,
but all property owners shall be conclusively
estopped and precluded from in any manner assail-
ing the effectiveness or validity thereof . Such bonds
shall convey and transfer to the owners thereof all
liens , right , title , and interest in and to the assess-

CHAPTER 135.
AN ACT to provide for the suppression of contagious diseases
among bees , to create the office of State Inspector of Apiaries
and to appropriate money therefor .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
In addition to the duties heretofore assigned to
him the State Entomologist is hereby appointed
State Inspector of Apiaries with power to appoint
from time and for such period or periods of time
as he may designate in said order or orders of ap-
pointed deputy inspectors as they may be repaired
in the interest of time , economy or in emergency
for inspection and treatment of Apiaries in different
parts of the State . The term " Inspector " when
used in this act includes deputy inspector unless
otherwise specified .
It shall be the duty of the State Inspector to aid
the development of the bee and honey industry and
to adopt proper measures for the prevention and
suppression of contagious and infectious diseases
among bees .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 401
Wherever the owner or owners of an apiary be-
lieve or have reason to believe that foul brood or
any other dangerous disease or diseases which is in-
fectious or contagious in its nature and injuries to
honey or bees in their egg, larval , pupal or adult
stages is present among their bees , they shall at
once notify the inspector .
The State Inspector shall have authority at his
discretion to visit and examine personally or by
deputy any apiary for the purpose of ascertaining
the existence or for the treatment or destruction
of any disease in the egg, larval , pupal or adult
stages among bees or brood ; and for this or any
other purpose within the scope of this act , he may
enter upon private property or premises during
reasonable business hours . When notified by any
one of the probable existence of such contagious or
infectious disease of bees or brood in any apiary ,
he shall visit and examine said apiary personally
or by deputy , as he shall deem most expedient with-
in ten days from receipt of said notice . Whenever
any such contagious or infectious disease of bees
or brood shall be found the inspector shall inspect
all apiaries within a radius of three miles of said
apiary of which he is able to learn by diligence .
After inspecting any apiaries of appurtenances
thereof of bees or brood in which any such contagi-
ous or infectious disease has been found , he shall
thoroughly disinfect his person and clothing before
leaving the premises where such disease is found.
All bees , brood , eggs or apiary appurtenances
infected or contaminated with infectious materials

cretion, may order the owner or the person in charge

CHAPTER 136 .
AN ACT relating to cities of sixth class .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That all cities of the sixth class shall have
the right to incur indebtedness not to exceed $4 , -
500.00 for fire apparatus .
2. The Board of Trustees of said sixth class
cities by a majority vote shall have the power to
incur said indebtedness .
§ 3. Whenever the Board of Trustees of cities of
the sixth class shall incur indebtedness for fire pro-
tection , there shall be levied annually a tax not to
exceed in any year , thirty cents on the one hundred
dollars which shall be placed in sinking funds for
redemption of said indebtedness .
4. All acts and parts of acts in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

7. Whenever a registered voter changes his or

aries of the precinct are changed by law , it shall be

tered therein as herein provided , and the registra-

Whenever any voter shall move from one county in
this State to another county in this State , such voter
shall apply to the County Court Clerk of the county
where he is registered directing the said Clerk to
cancel his name on the registration books and said
Clerk shall then deliver to the said voter a certificate
showing that his name has been cancelled on the
registration books . The voter then shall carry such
certificate into the county to which he is moving and
shall present said certificate to the officers of regis-
tration on the next registration day and the officers
of registration shall proceed to register him as pro-
vided in this Act and shall take up his certificate
and destroy it . The County Court Clerk of each
county shall keep a book arranged alphabetically
showing the names of the voters who have changed
their voting precincts , showing the precinct re-
moved from and the precinct removed to , and the
date of such transfer .

17. Any officer of registration , or other person ,

add any name thereto , or who shall willfully secrete ,
suppress or destroy any such book , or who shall
make or aid in making any false or fraudulent reg-
istration book , shall be deemed guilty of felony , and
shall be confined in the penitentiary not less than
one year nor more than five years , and shall forfeit
any office he then holds , and shall forever be disqual-
ified from holding office .
§ 18. Any person who , by himself or in aid of
others , shall forcibly break , or attempt to break up,
a registration held as provided in this article , or
shall forcibly prevent , or attempt to prevent , any
person from approaching or entering a place of reg-
istration for the purpose of registering , shall be
fined not less than one hunded nor more than five
hundred dollars , or imprisoned not less than six nor
more than twelve months in the county jail , or both .

RESOLUTION congratulating the people of Mexico , upon the

Mexico an era of strife and revolution in which life , liberty ,
property and the pursuit of happiness were at all times in
jeopardy ; and whereas , there has finally been elected to the
Presidency of our great sister Republic a man who promises to
be the true savior to his country , the most Honorable General
Alvaro Obregon ; and whereas , President Obregon , himself a
man of the people , enjoys the entire respect , confidence and en-
thusiastic backing of all classes in his country ; and whereas ,
a new era of peace , prosperity and friendly relationship has
been brought about by his excellency , President Obregon ; and ,
whereas , it is most fitting and appropriate that the great Com-
monwealth of Kentucky should join her sister states in a formal

of commendaton and confidence in President Obregon ; now ,
therefore ,

Whereas , the location of Camp Knox and its equipment has
made it the best artillery training school and firing range in the
United States ; and ,
Whereas, its location is but a few miles from the center of
population of the United States , and the climate such as to per-
mit outdoor training the year around ; and ,
Whereas , the purchase of said military site is more than ninety
(ninety per cent ) per cent completed ; and ,
Whereas , there is some indication as expressed in the public
press of the War Department abandoning Camp Knox ; now ,
therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the
House of Representatives concurring therein :
§ 1. That the Secretary of War and the Chief
of Staff for the Artillery Department of the United
States Army, as well as the National Congress , are
hereby respectively petitioned by the General As-
sembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky to retain
and not abandon Camp Knox of Kentucky as an
artillery training school and training ground for the
National Guard of the Fifth Corps Area ; and that
they be further petitioned to grant adequate sums
for the permanent improvement and equipment of
said Camp Knox to the end that it may become the
chief center of artillery training and for all branches
of the United States Army.
2. That certified copies of this Resolution be
transmitted by the Clerks of the Senate and House
respectively to the Secretary of War , Washington ,
D. C. , to the Chief of the Staff of the United States
Army , to General Snow, Chief of Artillery , and to
the chairman of each of the committees on Military
Affairs in the Senate and House of Representatives
of the National Congress , and to each Congressman
and Senator from Kentucky .
Approved January 25, 1922 .

CHAPTER 146.
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 11.
RESOLUTION extending thanks to the Chamber of Commerce
for courtesies extended the General Assembly .
Whereas , the Chamber of Commerce in the city of Frankfort ,
Kentucky , did on January sixteen , one thousand nine hundred
and twenty-two, entertain the General Assembly at the Young
Men's Christian Association building in said city, which was a
very enjoyable affair ; now, therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate , the House of Repre-
sentatives concurring therein:
That we extend to said Chamber of Commerce our
sincere thanks , and here express our appreciation
for said entertainment .
Approved January 25 , 1922 .

438 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Whereas , the State is not in possession of sufficiently definite
information as to the needs of the future ; and ,
Whereas , the future welfare of the State will be best sub-
served by a thorough investigation along this line ; and ,
Whereas , the National Committee for Mental Hygiene has
offered to make a survey of the State concerning the status of
the feeble -minded and epileptics , which survey would be similar
those they have made and are making in other states , and
which would involve no expense to the State except the use of
suitable rooms for office purposes ; and ,
Whereas, a study and survey of the conditions and needs
would materially assist the future legislature in determining a
practical policy to be pursued in this matter ; now , therefore ,
Be it resolved by the House of Representatives of
the State of Kentucky , the Senate concurring
therein :
That the National Committee for Mental Hygiene
be and are hereby requested to make a thorough in-
vestigation of the number and needs of the feeble-
minded and epileptic living within this State , and to
make a report of their investigation together with
their recommendations , to the Governor and the
next General Assembly ; and that the State Board
of Health of the State of Kentucky , be requested to
co-operate with said National Committee for Mental
Hygiene , furnishing to them any necessary informa-
tion in its possession . Resolved , further , that the
Custodian of Public Buildings shall furnish said Na-
tional Committee for Mental Hygiene such suitable
offices and furniture as may be necessary for the
time required in their work .
Approved February 27 , 1922 .

CHAPTER 152 .
SENATE RESOLUTION No. 18 .
RESOLUTION providing for the purchase of copies of the Leg-
islative Digest , adopted at the session of one thousand nine
hundred and twenty, as the official publication for the Gen-
eral Assembly , and certain officials .
Whereas , it is a matter of importance and convenience that
each Senator and Representative be advised as to the various
and detailed steps of legislation ; and ,
�
Whereas , this General Assembly for the past seven sessions
has approved and adopted as its official publication " The Leg-
islative Digest ," a publication under the editorship and man-
agement of competent and experienced persons , the purpose of
which is to fully advise from day to day the officials and public
of the Commonwealth as to the matter affecting them in an in-
telligent and prompt manner ; therefore ,
Be it resolved by the Senate of Kentucky , the
House of Representatives concurring :
That the publishers of the Legislative Digest are
hereby directed to furnish daily a copy to each mem-
ber and the clerks of the General Assembly , and to
the heads of each State Department during the time
of this session . That the publishers are allowed the
sum of ten dollars for each subscription for the ses-
sion and the Auditor of Public Accounts is directed
to draw his warrant upon the Treasurer for said
amount when such service has been concluded .
Approved March 6, 1922 .

CHAPTER 158 .
HOUSE RESOLUTION No. 21 .
RESOLUTION providing for furnishing Kentucky Directories .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
$ 1. That the State Librarian is hereby author-
ized and directed to purchase at not exceeding one
dollar and fifty cents per copy , five hundred copies
of the book entitled : " Kentucky Directory , one thou-
sand nine hundred and twenty-two , " for the use of
the courts , State and county officials and members
of the General Assembly of Kentucky . The Li-
brarian will furnish the members of the General As-
sembly and such officials as may be designated by
the presiding officers of the respective Housés , a
copy of the same and will furnish the State officials
with copies thereof and send a copy thereof to the
clerk of the county court of each county of the State ,
to be kept in his office . The Librarian will furnish
to the Librarian of each state and territory in the
Union , a copy of said book in exchange for similar
books from the said states and territories and a copy
to the Librarian of Congress .
$ 2. It being necessary that the officials should
have the books herein provided for, and emergency
is declared and this resolution shall take effect upon
its adoption and approval by the Governor .
Approved March 23 , 1922 .

RESOLUTION authorizing Theodore B. Blakey and Clayton B.
Blakey to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
Whereas , on April 11 , 1919 , the Governor of Kentucky , upon
the recommendation of the Attorney General of Kentucky , em-
ployed Theodore B. Blakey and Clayton B. Blakey to make cer-
tain investigations and perform certain legal services for the
State , which the Attorney General was unable to perform ; and ,
Whereas , the said Blakeys did under that contract . make ex-
tensive investigation and paid out in actual expenses in such
work the sum of $2,733.09 ; and ,
Whereas , on the 28th day of November , 1919 , in the case of
Commonwealth vs. Roberts Coal Company , the Court of Appeals
held that the Governor had the right to employ said Blakeys to
make said investigations but that the contract for the institu-
tion of suits was void on account of indefiniteness , the said
Blakeys were deprived of any opportunity to secure any com-
pensation or a return of the money which they had paid out in
making said investigations ; and
Whereas , in equity and good conscience such money so ex-
pended by them for the Commonwealth should be refunded by
it ; now, therefore ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Theodore B. Blakey and Clayton B. Blakey
be and they are hereby authorized to institute a suit
in the Franklin Circuit Court against the Common-
wealth of Kentucky to recover the amount of money
which they actually paid out as expenses in making
the investigations required by their contract of date

It establishes the Department of State Roads and Highways, outlines the powers and duties of its officers, sets the salary for the State Highway Engineer, and defines the department's expenditure limits. classification.title: An Act relating to roads and highways
classification.summary: This act authorizes the State Highway Commission to prepare plans, enter into contracts, and supervise the construction of Federal aid roads. It also outlines the process for the State Treasurer to receive federal funds and for the Auditor to issue warrants for payments. The act further details the transfer of roads constructed under the State aid law to respective counties, with the counties being responsible for maintenance unless the roads are part of the primary system. The State is bound to pay its share of construction costs, with annual payments to counties capped at the amount received for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1920.
